metadata.summary: Adopt W3C DTCG format for vendor-neutral token portability across Figma, Style Dictionary, and platforms without lossy remapping.

content.title: W3C Design Tokens Standard
content.summary: A methodology for authoring design tokens that conform to the W3C Design Tokens Community Group (DTCG) format module — using `$type`, `$value`, `$description`, and `{alias}` references — so that the same token file can round-trip between Figma, Style Dictionary v4, and native platforms without lossy remapping.

why.p: Every non-standard token format (custom Style Dictionary JSON, Theo, Tokens Studio) creates a translation layer that loses type information, breaks aliases, or produces platform-specific names. The DTCG format is the emerging interoperability standard already implemented by Tokens Studio, Specify, Supernova, Penpot, and Style Dictionary v4. Adopting it now prevents a costly migration when tooling locks in and prevents cross-tool collaboration on token ownership.

list.item: Authoring a token source-of-truth that must round-trip between Figma and code platforms.
Migrating a legacy Style Dictionary, Theo, or Tokens Studio file to vendor-neutral DTCG JSON.
Setting up a shared token pipeline across web, iOS, Android, or React Native.
Preparing for tooling that implements DTCG: Style Dictionary v4, Tokens Studio, Specify, Penpot.
Single-platform single-app project — DTCG overhead is not justified; CSS custom properties suffice.
Fast spike or throwaway prototype — schema discipline slows iteration with no payoff.
Team with no token naming discipline yet — fix primitives/semantic split first (`token-organization`).

rule.statement: Every token must have both `$type` and `$value`; include `$description` for all tokens that will be consumed by other teams or platforms.
rule.rationale: Missing `$type` prevents tools from generating correct platform outputs (e.g., px vs pt vs dp); missing `$description` makes the token file unmaintainable as the team grows.

list.item: `$type` — the DTCG type string: "color", "dimension", "fontFamily", "fontWeight", "duration", "cubicBezier", "number", "shadow", "gradient", "typography", "transition".
`$value` — the token value in the canonical format for its type.
`$description` — human-readable note on the token's purpose (optional but strongly recommended).
`$extensions` — vendor-specific extensions under a namespaced key (e.g., "org.brand.source").

rule.statement: Use dot-notation alias references (`{group.subgroup.token}`) to create semantic tokens that point to primitives; never hard-code the same raw value in two places.
rule.rationale: Duplicated raw values break theming — changing a primitive requires updating every duplicate; aliases let one change propagate.

list.item: Gradients, typography composites, and motion still vary across tool implementations — expect tool-specific `$extensions` for these.
The spec is a Community Group draft, not a W3C Recommendation; breaking changes between format versions still occur.
Math expressions and reference loops are not detected by the spec — resolver tools must catch cycles at build time.

rule.statement: Run a JSON schema validator against the DTCG format module schema before committing any token file; never trust agent-generated JSON to be schema-valid without validation.
rule.rationale: LLMs silently invent `$type` values that no parser accepts (e.g., "px", "hex"); schema validation catches these before they break downstream platforms.

rule.statement: Treat alias references as the only permitted way to link semantic tokens to primitives; never inline a primitive's raw value into a semantic token.
rule.rationale: Inlined raw values break theming; a theme switch requires updating every inlined value rather than repointing a single alias.

rule.statement: Validate that no alias circular references exist before running Style Dictionary or any resolver.
rule.rationale: The DTCG spec does not detect cycles; circular references produce silent infinite loops or build failures in downstream tools.

antipattern.description: Agent emits unresolved alias references (e.g., `{color.brand.primary}`) in the final output file without verifying the referenced token exists.
antipattern.reason: Broken aliases cause Style Dictionary builds to fail or silently produce empty values; validate the resolved token map before commit.

antipattern.description: Agent applies DTCG format to a single-app project with fewer than 50 tokens.
antipattern.reason: DTCG format overhead (schema, tooling, resolver) is not justified for small, single-platform projects; CSS custom properties in one file are sufficient.

antipattern.description: Agent normalizes an existing Tokens Studio file to DTCG without running `token-organization` first.
antipattern.reason: Format migration does not fix structural problems (raw-value naming, no semantic layer); applying DTCG to a poorly organized set produces a well-formatted mess.

list.item: Tokens Studio for Figma — DTCG import/export.
Style Dictionary v4 — native DTCG support with platform transformers.
Specify, Supernova, Penpot — DTCG-based sync.
schema validator: `https://design-tokens.org/schema.json`.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: Treat DTCG as the canonical artifact. Have one subagent extract tokens from existing source (Figma export, SCSS variables, Tailwind config), a second normalize them to DTCG with a strict JSON schema validator in the loop, and a third generate platform outputs via Style Dictionary v4. Always run schema validation before committing — LLMs silently invent `$type` values that no parser accepts.

list.item: `ui-token-extractor` — pulls raw values from Figma REST API or CSS/SCSS source.
`dtcg-normalizer` — rewrites raw values into DTCG groups with `$type`/`$value`/`$description`; validates against schema.
`style-dictionary-builder` — runs `style-dictionary build` and verifies platform outputs (CSS, iOS, Android) compile.
Pin to a specific draft version in `$schema` and CI; the spec moves and silent breakage is common.
Keep primitives in one file, semantic aliases in another, component overrides in a third — mirrors `token-organization` hierarchy and reduces merge conflicts.
Use `$description` aggressively — it survives transforms and becomes inline docs in Storybook.
Place tool-specific data under `$extensions.<tool>` not at the root; keeps the file portable.
Always emit a `tokens.lock.json` snapshot of resolved values; downstream consumers diff against it to catch silent renames.

LLMs invent fictional `$type` values (`shadow-x`, `gradient-linear`). Constrain to the spec list (color, dimension, fontFamily, fontWeight, duration, cubicBezier, number, strokeStyle, border, transition, shadow, gradient, typography) in the prompt.
Models add trailing commas and JS-style comments to JSON; require validators with strict JSON parsing.
Aliases are easy to write but hard to reason about — agents create cycles. Have a deterministic cycle-check step before the LLM finalizes the file.
"Composite" tokens (typography, shadow) trip up agents: they emit nested `$value` objects with bare strings instead of typed dimensions. Force `$type: "dimension"` plus `$value: { value, unit }`.
Human checkpoint: after first DTCG generation, designer reviews semantic alias map manually. Wrong aliases propagate into every platform output.

template.purpose: Minimal DTCG-compliant token file with color, spacing, and typography groups
